  "opinion": "I don't think that applies to me directly because I actually don't use much of the social media stuff. But from what I understand, it's really tough out there with all this fake news floating around online. As an electrician in New York who was born in another country and is now settled here with my husband—we have one child together—I can say that staying informed without relying on those platforms sounds better these days. Growing up with parents who were educated at least to upper secondary levels helped me develop a good way of thinking critically, even though my formal education stopped there too.\n\nDealing with depression makes it hard for me personally to cope well with anything, let alone something like social media that can be overwhelming and sometimes distressing. But since I don't really engage much anyway through my work or hobbies—photography keeps me busy outdoors, running helps clear my head, football is a fun team sport we enjoy occasionally when the weather's good, gardening gives me peace—I find myself avoiding all that fake content nonsense entirely.\n\nSo while others might struggle with managing misinformation online based on their experiences and personalities, mine are simple: don't look too much; use common sense from life experience.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I don't think that applies to me directly because I actually don't use much of the social media stuff. But from what I understand, it's really tough out there with all this fake news floating around online. As an electrician in New York who was born in another country and is now settled here with my husband—we have one child together—I can say that staying informed without relying on those platforms sounds better these days. Growing up with parents who were educated at least to upper secondary levels helped me develop a good way of thinking critically, even though my formal education stopped there too.

Dealing with depression makes it hard for me personally to cope well with anything, let alone something like social media that can be overwhelming and sometimes distressing. But since I don't really engage much anyway through my work or hobbies—photography keeps me busy outdoors, running helps clear my head, football is a fun team sport we enjoy occasionally when the weather's good, gardening gives me peace—I find myself avoiding all that fake content nonsense entirely.

So while others might struggle with managing misinformation online based on their experiences and personalities, mine are simple: don't look too much; use common sense from life experience.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
First, I considered the persona provided—specifically her age (62), which might mean she's been exposed to different eras of communication.
Next, as someone married with children and self-employed after migrating from another place under Hindu beliefs but not depressed enough for severe avoidance due to low neuroticism scores,
her approach is influenced by practicality over emotional overload.
